# AI policy

We build with AI, and we want you to as well. Coding agents, autocomplete, and chat assistants are genuinely useful
tools, and using them to write, refactor, debug, and *learn* is encouraged across every Bitwise Media Group repository.

But a tool is only as good as the person driving it. This policy exists to keep the bar high while keeping the door
wide open: **use AI all you like, as long as you understand and can stand behind what you submit.**

## The critical rule

**You must understand your code.**

If you cannot explain what a change does and how it interacts with the rest of the system *without* the help of an AI
tool, it is not ready to submit. That is the whole rule — everything else here follows from it.

This isn't anti-AI; it's how we keep our projects maintainable and how you actually get better. Use an agent with
access to the codebase, interrogate it, read what it produces, and keep going until you genuinely grasp the change and
its edge cases. *Then* open the pull request.

## You are accountable, not the AI

When you [sign off and sign your commits](CONTRIBUTING.md#signing-and-signing-off-every-commit), you certify that the
work is yours to submit under our [MIT licence](../LICENSE). "The AI wrote it" is not a defence for code you don't
understand, a bug you can't explain, or a licence you can't honour — the human in the loop owns the result.

Reviewers spend real effort on every pull request. Submitting AI output you haven't read or understood pushes that cost
onto a maintainer, and it's the quickest way to erode trust (and have your contribution closed).

## Learn as you go

Treat AI as a tutor, not an oracle. Ask it *why*, not just *what*. Every contribution should leave behind two things: a
better project, and a contributor who understands a little more than they did yesterday. If you finish a change having
learned nothing from it, you've used the tool wrong.

## In practice

Encouraged:

- Using AI to draft, refactor, explain, or debug code that you then read, test, and understand.
- Pairing with an agent to explore the codebase and learn how the pieces fit together.
- Using AI to tighten the wording of issues, docs, and pull request descriptions.

Not OK:

- Opening a pull request — or an issue or review — you couldn't write up or defend on your own.
- Pasting agent output unread and hoping a maintainer will sort out the rest.
- Contributing AI-generated media (art, logos, audio, and so on) without agreeing it with a maintainer first; these
  carry brand and licensing concerns we handle case by case.

If a change is substantially AI-generated, just say so in the pull request. It isn't a mark against you — it tells
reviewers where to look closely, and that kind of honesty is exactly what the critical rule is built on.
